title: Brent Hits $100 as EU Carves Out Russian LNG Shipping, Black Sea Drones Halt Kazakh Oil, India Reaches for Myanmar Rare Earths
subhead: Oil surged past $100/bbl as Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers opened a second front in the Red Sea, the Black Sea pipeline corridor came under Ukrainian drone fire, and EU leaders struck a compromise sanctions package that exempted Greek Russian LNG shipping — exposing the limits of European energy coercion against Moscow.

sections.heading: Brent Crosses $100 as Three Chokepoints Squeeze Simultaneously
sections.body: Brent crude surged past $100 a barrel on Thursday, the highest since May, after Houthi missile and drone strikes hit two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea and the US launched its 13th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. The Houthi-declared naval blockade of Saudi Arabia in the Bab el-Mandeb strait — the Red Sea chokepoint through which roughly 12% of the world's seaborne oil previously flowed — turned what was already a two-front squeeze into a three-front crisis. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, the Bab el-Mandeb is now under active attack, and Ukrainian drone strikes on tankers near Novorossiysk have suspended loadings at the CPC terminal in the Black Sea. According to the Wall Street Journal, the three chokepoints collectively imperil roughly a quarter of global oil supply. Insurance costs for shipping through the southern Red Sea doubled for some companies on Thursday, per Reuters. US crude inventories — including the SPR — fell by 3 million barrels in the week ending July 17 to the lowest level since the Reagan administration, while OECD government-controlled stocks dropped 44 million barrels in June from May's already-depleted levels, the lowest since December 1990.
sections.whyItMatters: The simultaneous closure of three major energy chokepoints is unprecedented in modern oil market history. With strategic reserves at multi-decade lows, the buffer for further disruption is thin.

sections.heading: Houthi Strikes on Saudi Tankers Close the Red Sea Alternative
sections.body: Yemen's Houthi forces struck two Saudi oil tankers — the Encelia and the Layla — in the Red Sea on Wednesday night, with the Saudi-flagged Encelia transmitting a distress call reporting a missile strike near Jizan that caused a fire at the bow, according to Saudi state media. Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree said the attacks were carried out because the vessels 'violated the blockade decision.' The Houthis claim to have forced at least nine ships to turn back from Bab el-Mandeb since declaring a Saudi naval blockade earlier this week. The attack is significant because Saudi Arabia had redirected millions of barrels per day through the Red Sea via its East-West pipeline to Yanbu precisely to bypass the Hormuz closure. That alternative route is now under fire. President Trump vowed 'major military punishment' for Iran and the Houthis, saying the US would hold Iran responsible for Houthi attacks. He told Axios Iran 'hasn't received enough pain yet' and was considering relaunching major combat operations. Meanwhile, two Chinese-operated tankers — the Xin Long Yang and Cosnew Lake, both loaded with Saudi crude at Yanbu — continued toward Bab el-Mandeb, openly broadcasting Chinese destination signals, Bloomberg reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The Houthi blockade transforms the Red Sea from a safety valve into a second front. Saudi Arabia's pipeline workaround for Hormuz is now directly targeted, leaving Riyadh with no secure maritime export route.

sections.heading: Black Sea Drone Strikes Halt Kazakh Crude at CPC Terminal
sections.body: Ukrainian drone strikes hit multiple commercial tankers near the Caspian Pipeline Consortium terminal at Novorossiysk over a four-day stretch in mid-July, forcing repeated suspensions of loading operations. At least four ships sustained damage, including the Nordic Zenith and NELSA, which were struck while loading or preparing to load Kazakh crude. A Chevron-chartered tanker owned by Greek shipper Aristidis Alafouzos was also damaged; Alafouzos warned the attacks are 'a systematic threat to the arteries of the world's energy supply.' Kazakhstan — which gets roughly 90% of its export revenue from oil and sends about 80% of its crude through the CPC pipeline — has cut production and is seeking compensation for the disruptions. The CPC pipeline handles over 1% of global oil supply. Three western oil developments in Kazakhstan have reduced output as the export route via Russia halted operations after the drone strikes, Upstream Online reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's Black Sea campaign has added a third chokepoint to the global energy map. Kazakhstan — not a party to either conflict — is now collateral damage, and the involvement of Chevron-chartered tankers draws American commercial interests directly into the line of fire.

sections.heading: EU Adopts 21st Russia Sanctions Package With Greek LNG Carve-Out
sections.body: The European Union adopted its 21st package of sanctions against Russia on Thursday, targeting banks, cryptocurrency networks, oil traders, and the shadow fleet. But the package arrived with a telling compromise: Greece secured a temporary exemption for certain shipping activities related to Russian LNG destined for third countries. Greece, one of the world's largest maritime nations, argued that tougher restrictions on Russian LNG shipping would hurt European shipowners more than Russia's energy revenues — exposing what OilPrice analyst Cyril Widdershoven called the structural flaw in Europe's sanctions approach. The EU has focused on import restrictions while Russian hydrocarbons have simply been redirected to China, India, and Turkey via European-owned and operated shipping networks. The package also comes as Russia banned diesel exports on July 8 following domestic fuel shortages caused by Ukrainian drone strikes on refineries. Russian crude processing rates fell to their lowest in over two decades, averaging less than 4 million barrels per day this month, according to EA Analytics.
sections.whyItMatters: The Greek carve-out reveals the limits of European sanctions: the infrastructure that moves Russian energy to global markets is still largely European-owned. Sanctions that don't restrict the logistics chain merely redirect trade, not reduce it.

sections.heading: US-Saudi Nuclear Deal Draws Proliferation Criticism and South Korean Ire
sections.body: US Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman formally signed a civil nuclear cooperation agreement — a so-called 123 agreement — on Wednesday, paving the way for Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium on its own soil using US technology. The deal, described as a 'decades-long, multi-billion-dollar' partnership, notably does not require the snap UN inspections that Washington has demanded from Iran. Nonproliferation experts immediately warned it could spark a regional arms race. President Trump then added a condition on Thursday, saying the deal requires Saudi Arabia to normalize relations with Israel under the Abraham Accords — a step Riyadh has so far refused. The deal has drawn sharp criticism from South Korea, which has long sought US permission to enrich uranium for its own nuclear program and sees a double standard: Seoul, a treaty ally, is denied what Washington grants Riyadh. The New York Times reported that the arrangement exposes a double standard in US nuclear policy. The agreement requires congressional review, where opposition is expected.
sections.whyItMatters: Washington is granting uranium enrichment rights to the same Gulf monarchy that fought Iran's nuclear program for decades — while denying those rights to its own treaty allies. The geopolitical signal is as significant as the proliferation risk.

sections.heading: India-Myanmar Rare Earth Pact Targets China's Processing Monopoly
sections.body: India and Myanmar have agreed to deepen cooperation in the rare earths sector, with Indian teams visiting Myanmar to assess mining cooperation, Reuters reported. New Delhi's push aims to reduce dependence on China, which currently controls roughly 90% of global rare earth refining and processing capacity. Myanmar's rare earth mines — located primarily in Kachin State near the Chinese border — have been dominated by Chinese operators for years. An ISP-Myanmar study found that mining sites are deeply integrated with China's processing ecosystem. India's interest comes as the US Trump administration signed a July 20 executive order giving defense contractors until January 1, 2027 to remove Chinese, Russian, Iranian, and North Korean critical minerals from their supply chains — a deadline IndustryWeek called potentially unworkable, noting that 'you cannot legislate a smelter into existence.' The US imports zero titanium sponge domestically and relies on Japan for 44,000 tons annually, while China controls the midstream processing that turns rare earth ore into usable material. Korea's Financial Services Commission also announced plans to use the National Growth Fund to stabilize critical mineral supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: The rare earth challenge is not mining — it's processing. India's Myanmar gambit and the US executive order both target the same bottleneck, but neither addresses the years of smelter and refinery construction needed to break China's midstream monopoly.

sections.heading: Gulf States Accelerate Pipeline Bypasses as Hormuz Alternative Proves Vulnerable
sections.body: At least seven major pipeline projects are under construction, in planning, or under discussion to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, CBS News reported, citing government officials, oil companies, and analysts. Before the war, roughly 15 million barrels per day of Persian Gulf oil transited Hormuz. Saudi Arabia's existing East-West pipeline (Abqaiq to Yanbu) and the UAE's Habshan-Fujairah pipeline are now running near full capacity, with combined spare capacity of 3.5 to 5.5 million bpd now largely consumed. Abu Dhabi's state oil company is accelerating construction of a $3 billion, 200-mile parallel pipeline to Fujairah that would add over 1.2 million bpd of capacity. Kpler estimates completion by mid-2027. Iraq, which gets 90% of its revenue from oil and is heavily dependent on Hormuz, is pursuing pipelines to Ceyhan, Turkey (with a branch to Baniyas, Syria) that could carry roughly 2 million bpd. But Thursday's Houthi attacks demonstrated that even Red Sea routes are now vulnerable, meaning no single bypass can guarantee security. As Kpler's Victoria Grabenwöger noted, relying on Hormuz 'is no longer a prudent long-term strategy' — but diversification itself is now under fire.
sections.whyItMatters: Gulf states are spending billions on pipeline alternatives, but the Houthi Red Sea blockade has shown that geographic diversification alone doesn't solve the problem when both exit routes face armed disruption.

bottomLine: Oil at $100 reflects a market waking up to the reality that three of the world's most critical energy chokepoints — Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb, and the Black Sea — are simultaneously under threat, with strategic reserves at their lowest in decades. The Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers close off the Red Sea workaround that had been Gulf states' main relief valve for the Hormuz closure. The EU's 21st sanctions package, with its Greek LNG shipping carve-out, reveals the persistent gap between European political intent and commercial reality: sanctions that exempt the logistics chain don't reduce Russian energy revenue, they redirect it. The US-Saudi nuclear deal, while conditional on Israel normalization, grants uranium enrichment rights to Riyadh that Washington denies its own allies, raising proliferation concerns at the exact moment it's bombing Iran over nuclear fears. And beneath it all, the critical mineral supply chain challenge — from India's Myanmar outreach to the Pentagon's January 2027 deadline — is running up against the same stubborn fact: processing capacity takes years to build, and legislation can't accelerate smelters.
sources: OilPrice.com, CBS News, USA Today, Reuters, Bloomberg, Wall Street Journal (via LiveMint), IndustryWeek, Defense News, Times of India (via Firstpost), Time, CryptoBriefing, Upstream Online, TradeWinds, New York Times, MSN, BBC, Al Jazeera, Council of the EU, Kpler, EA Analytics, Vortexa
